design_philosophy.core_loop: Clear single-screen arenas of waves of enemies → collect money/prizes/power-ups → choose exit to next arena → repeat with escalating difficulty
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Survival under enemy pressure while balancing offensive positioning and collection of valuable items; cooperative competition in two-player mode
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Game show contestant surviving and profiting in a dystopian televised death game; emphasized by constant announcer commentary ('Big money! Big prizes!') and prize collection mechanics
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Intentionally arcade-centric design philosophy: relies on continue mechanic and quarter-feeding rather than forgiving progression; treats game completion as aspirational (developers initially unsure players would finish); embraces spectacle and satirical game-show framing over narrative depth; twin-stick shooter archetype with wave-based enemy spawning over simultaneous visible threats

difficulty.continues_system: Arcade game designed to lean on continue payment; home conversions likely adapted this
difficulty.checkpointing: Single-screen arena completion acts as checkpoint; map reveals level structure after first arena; no backtracking allowed
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Exit choice after arena completion provides limited route variation; two-player competitive collection reward structure creates secondary difficulty incentive

progression.upgrade_system: Temporary power-ups and weapons collected during arena combat; no permanent progression mechanics documented
progression.unlock_mechanism: Key collection throughout levels unlocks Pleasure Dome bonus level (added post-release due to player demand); map-gated progression through selected exits
replayability_hooks: Two-player competitive score rivalry (extra reward for highest cash collector per level)
Pleasure Dome bonus level unlocked via key collection

world_building_techniques: Announcer voice lines punctuating gameplay ('Total carnage! I love it!', 'Big money! Big prizes!')
Dystopian game-show setting framing (1999 future, violent television spectacle)
Sadistic host character who appears as final boss
Thematic borrowing from RoboCop and The Running Man (1987) for tone

boss_design.philosophy: End-of-level culmination encounters; sadistic host serves as final boss, thematically tied to game-show premise
notable_bosses: Sadistic host (final boss, integrated into narrative)

faction_indicators: Game show contestant vs. establishment (violent dystopian spectacle)
Player competition in two-player mode (cash/prize rivalry)
danger_signals: Satirical commodification of violence ('the most violent game show of all time')
Prize mechanics treating human figures (bikini-clad 'babes') as collectible objects
environmental_storytelling: Single-screen arena design conveys containment within game-show stage
Money and prize piles scattered throughout space reinforce profiteering motivation
Four-edge enemy entrances suggest manufactured waves of opposition
Blocked backtracking enforces forward momentum (no escape route)
